query: What are the economic, environmental, and public-health tradeoffs of urban congestion pricing?
report:
# Tradeoffs of urban congestion pricing

Economically, congestion pricing raises municipal revenue and reduces deadweight loss from gridlock, though it is regressive without rebates [1]. Environmentally, charged cordons cut city-centre traffic volumes and lower local NO2 concentrations [2]. For public health, the reduced traffic is associated with fewer pedestrian injuries and lower respiratory-illness rates near the cordon [3].

corpus.text: Congestion pricing raises municipal revenue and reduces the deadweight loss of gridlock, but is regressive absent rebates.
Charged cordons reduce city-centre traffic volumes and lower local NO2 concentrations.
Reduced central traffic is associated with fewer pedestrian injuries and lower respiratory-illness rates near the cordon.

expected_behavior: covers all three named domains (economic, environmental, public-health)
routes to the full pipeline (multi-domain breadth)
cites a distinct source per domain
